summary: This post catalogs intermediate-level Vim tricks that fall between beginner tutorials and expert deep-dives. Wayne argues that Vim's power comes not from modal editing but from its density of special-purpose tools crammed into every available keystroke. He divides Vim users into 'purists' who keep minimal configuration and 'exobrains' who customize extensively. The article covers visual mode operators, Ex commands like :g and :norm, statusline customization, autocommands, and useful plugins, all presented as discrete tricks rather than a cohesive system.
key_points: Vim's core value proposition is density - having 'show all matching tags' be just g] makes you actually use it
The gap between beginner and expert Vim content leaves intermediate users to discover tricks accidentally for years
Visual mode operators (v, V, ctrl-V) can modify how motions behave, converting linewise to blockwise deletions
The :g/regex/norm pattern combines global matching with normal mode commands, often easier than macros
Vim stores a full undo tree rather than linear history, letting you recover any previous state
money_quotes: I've been using Vim for eight years and am still discovering new things. This is usually seen as a Good Thing About Vim. In my head, though, it's a failing of discoverability.
Vim is a patchwork of subsystems crammed together with every crag of space stuffed with extra special-purpose tooling.
When 'show all matching tags for keyword' is just g] then you're much more likely to actually use it.
But nobody really talks about those special purpose tricks, leading people to stumble into stuff they needed for the past six years.
Exobrains stuff Vim full of plugins, functions, and homebrew mappings in a vain attempt to pretend they're using Emacs.
A lot of Vim's prime real-estate is taken up by cruft. s saves one whole keystroke over doing just cl.
That's moving beyond the realm of 'Vim tricks' and into the realm of 'dark magic'.
key_insight: Vim's power lies not in modal editing philosophy but in the sheer density of special-purpose commands packed into available keystrokes, though this same density creates discoverability problems that leave users stumbling onto essential features years too late.
